author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
The Beginning of a Good Will is the Gift of Grace.
title: The Beginning of a Good Will is the Gift of Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Chapter 37 [XIX.]—The Beginning of a Good Will is the Gift of Grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But you think that a man is so aided by the grace of God in a good work, that in stirring up his will to that very good work you believe that grace does nothing; for this your own words sufficiently declare.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For why have you not said that a man is incited by God’s grace to a good work, as you have said that he is incited to evil by the suggestions of the devil, but have said that in a good work he is always aided by God’s grace?—as if by his own will, and without any grace of God, he undertook a good work, and were then divinely assisted in the work itself, for the sake, that is to say, of the merits of his good will; so that grace is rendered as due,—not given as not due,—and thus grace is made no more grace.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But this is what, in the Palestinian judgment, Pelagius with a deceitful heart condemned,—that the grace of God, namely, is given according to our merits.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Tell me, I beseech you, what good, Paul, while he was as yet Saul, willed, and not rather great evils, when breathing out slaughter he went, in horrible darkness of mind and madness, to lay waste the Christians?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For what merits of a good will did God convert him by a marvellous and sudden calling from those evils to good things?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: What shall I say, when he himself cries, “Not by works of righteousness that we have done, but according to His mercy He saved us”?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: What is that which I have already mentioned as having been said by the Lord, “No one can come to me,”—which is understood as “believe on me,”—unless it were given him of my Father”?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Whether is this given to him who is already willing to believe, for the sake of the merits of a good will? or rather is the will itself, as in the case of Saul, stirred up from above, that he may believe, even although he is so averse from the faith as even to persecute the believers?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For how has the Lord commanded us to pray for those who persecute us?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Do we pray thus that the grace of God may be recompensed them for the sake of their good will, and not rather that the evil will itself may be changed into a good one?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Just as we believe that at that time the saints whom he was persecuting did not pray for Saul in vain, that his will might be converted to the faith which he was destroying.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And indeed that his conversion was effected from above, appeared even by a manifest miracle.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But how many enemies of Christ are at the present day suddenly drawn by God’s secret grace to Christ!

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And if I had not set down this word from the gospel, what things would that man have said in this behalf concerning me, since even now he is stirring, not against me, but against Him who cries, “No man can come to me, except the Father who hath sent me draw him”!

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For He does not say, “except He lead him,” so that we can thus in any way understand that his will precedes.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For who is “drawn,” if he was already willing?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And yet no man comes unless he is willing.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Therefore he is drawn in wondrous ways to will, by Him who knows how to work within the very hearts of men.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Not that men who are unwilling should believe, which cannot be, but that they should be made willing from being unwilling.
